body_txt: Celestial Theme Naming is the practice of connecting characters by naming them after celestial figures — stars, constellations, planets, galaxies, and other such entities and phenomena. These are sometimes themed around specific sets of objects or figures, such as Zodiacal constellations, the planets of the Solar System, or major stars in the night sky. Other sets draw more widely for inspiration — a group of characters called Stellar, Galaxoid, Nebular, Planetar, and Asteroid would also fit this trope, for instance. This theme naming is generally associated with characters tied to the night or to outer space in some manner. Groups of aliens or other space-borne characters may have names of this sort to emphasize their otherworldly origins, and hero teams with powers drawn from or themed around stars and space will also usually choose stellar monikers for themselves. Cultures that place importance on astronomy or astrology are also likely to draw their names from what they see in the night sky. Note that when characters or entities are themselves living celestial figures, such as Sentient Stars or a Living Constellation , this trope does not apply. When Sirius or Ursa Major are living beings, it is no great wonder that they're going to be called that. A Sub-Trope of Scientific and Technological Theme Naming . See also Cosmic Motifs and Zodiac Motifs . For characters with a sun/moon duality or contrast specifically, see Solar and Lunar . This can also overlap with Religious and Mythological Theme Naming when names involve stars, constellations, and other celestial figures associated with deities or mythology. In Japanese works, it can overlap with "Day of the Week" Name . Note that, since many celestial objects were named after deities, characters with celestial or mythological theme naming may happen to be called the same or similar things, but would not necessarily be examples of both tropes; when deciding which cases to put where, be careful to note whether their other traits are referencing myths, celestial bodies, or both.
